topic: An employee discovers the company is being sold
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elias stared at the glossy brochure lying on his desk, the logo of the acquiring firm gleaming mockingly under the harsh office lights. He picked up a pen, his knuckles white against the plastic casing, and began methodically tapping it against the wood. Every tick of the pen felt like a countdown. He’d seen the internal memos, the hushed conversations in the breakroom—the casual way executives spoke about "synergies" and "streamlining." Now, the reality hit him, cold and sharp: they weren't just talking about growth; they were selling the whole damn operation. A slow burn started in his chest, tightening with a familiar, bitter knot. He pushed the brochure away, the sound of the paper rustling seeming deafening in the sudden silence of his workspace. He just wanted to pack up his things and walk out, but the thought of leaving felt too monumental, too draining.

[story 2]

“Another round of mandatory team-building exercises,” Sarah muttered, swirling the lukewarm coffee in her mug. She watched Mark across the cubicle wall, his usual easy smile stretched thin, almost brittle. She’d pieced together enough fragments—the sudden influx of high-level consultants, the strangely enthusiastic chatter about future ventures that never seemed to involve the actual work they did. A deep, simmering frustration rose in her throat, a pressure she had to physically fight to keep from erupting. She picked at a loose thread on her sweater, her jaw clenching so tightly her temples throbbed. This whole environment felt like a cage being dismantled piece by piece, and she felt utterly powerless to stop the demolition crew.

[story 3]

“So, what’s the plan for the Q3 restructuring?” David asked, his tone unnervingly cheerful as he leaned over the partition. He was talking about spreadsheets and projections, but the underlying implication hung heavy in the air like smog. Lena felt a distinct hollowness spreading through her stomach. She kept her gaze fixed on the monitor, refusing to meet his eyes, focusing instead on the faint flicker of the screen. Every time he spoke of 'optimizing assets,' a distinct wave of sourness washed over her, making her shoulders involuntarily slump. It was exhausting, this constant performance of compliance, knowing that the foundation beneath her feet was about to be ripped out. She just wanted the noise to stop.
